coredata.dc:title: Enhancing oil shale pyrolysis using metal-doped carbon quantum dot catalysts: A comprehensive behavioral, kinetic, and mechanistic analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Li
Guo, Jixiang
Zhou, Yu
Zhang, Xiaojun
Gao, Chenhao
Xiong, Ruiying
Song, Hanxuan
Chen, Xiangwei
Luo, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: Current oil shale extraction technologies face significant challenges, including high heating costs, slow pyrolysis reaction rates, and low efficiency, which hinder effective resource exploitation. To address these issues, this study synthesized three types of metal-doped carbon quantum dot (M/CQD) catalysts—Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs—and tested their effects on the pyrolysis of oil shale. The introduction of these catalysts resulted in a notable enhancement of the pyrolysis process, reducing the required activation energy by approximately 40 % and lowering the initiation temperatures for Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs by 64 °C, 62 °C, and 94 °C, respectively. Consequently, the yield of shale oil increased significantly by 23.1 %, 13.3 %, and 19.8 % for each catalyst. Kinetic and product analyses indicate that these catalysts facilitate electron transfer from organic molecules, promoting the formation of free radicals and the cleavage of C–C bonds. This enhancement leads to an increased production of short-chain hydrocarbons and a reduction in oxygenates and esters, thereby improving the quality of the shale oil. Further insights from Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations suggest that the catalysts enhance pyrolysis by promoting the formation of carbocations and synergizing with radicals released from the shale, intensifying the pyrolysis process. This study not only elucidates the catalytic mechanisms that enhance oil shale pyrolysis but also offers a promising approach to overcome technological challenges in shale resource recovery, such as high heating costs and low efficiency, paving the way for more efficient and cleaner shale oil production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: M/CQDs
Oil shale
Catalytic pyrolysis
DFT calculation
Product characteristics
Mechanism analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of pyrolytic behavior and kinetics
Analysis of oil shale pyrolysis products
DFT calculations and molecular dynamics simulations

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagrammatic representation of the oil shale pyrolysis furnace.
Synthesis roadmap (a) and TEM (b) of M/CQDs catalysts.
XRD of M/CQDs (a); FT-IR of M/CQDs (b); Zeta potential maps of M/CQDs (c); Raman spectra of M/CQDs (d); UV absorption spectra of M/CQDs (e); Physical image of M/CQDs fluorescence under UV light (f).
The TG-DTG analyses of shale under transition metal salts (a, b) and M/CQDs (c, d).
Shale oil production rates under different catalytic conditions.
Evaluation of gas generated through pyrolysis under varying conditions.
FT-IR spectrum of pyrolyzed shale semi-coke (a); shale oil at 500 °C under different catalytic conditions (b); Elemental analysis of shale oil (c); carbon number distribution of shale oil (d).
GC/MS ion spectrum of shale oil.
Effect of M/CQDs catalysts on the distribution characteristics of shale oil fractions (a), heterocyclic compounds (b), saturated (c) and unsaturated (d) hydrocarbons from pyrolysis products.
Construction of models (a); Schematic diagram of differential potential effect (b); Molecular dynamics simulations of M/CQDs and kerogen (c).
Simplified model of kerogen and model of Mn/CQDs clusters (a); reaction process diagram (b); reaction Gibbs free energy change (c).
The proximate and ultimate analysis of Changqing oil shale (wt.%).
Ash analysis of Changqing oil shale (wt.%).
Yield of aluminum retort products from different samples at 500°C.
Kinetic parameters of oil shale with M/CQDs using F-W-O method.

head.ce:title: Enhancing oil shale pyrolysis using metal-doped carbon quantum dot catalysts: A comprehensive behavioral, kinetic, and mechanistic analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Science and Technology, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Science and Technology, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Current oil shale extraction technologies face significant challenges, including high heating costs, slow pyrolysis reaction rates, and low efficiency, which hinder effective resource exploitation. To address these issues, this study synthesized three types of metal-doped carbon quantum dot (M/CQD) catalysts—Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs—and tested their effects on the pyrolysis of oil shale. The introduction of these catalysts resulted in a notable enhancement of the pyrolysis process, reducing the required activation energy by approximately 40 % and lowering the initiation temperatures for Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs by 64 °C, 62 °C, and 94 °C, respectively. Consequently, the yield of shale oil increased significantly by 23.1 %, 13.3 %, and 19.8 % for each catalyst. Kinetic and product analyses indicate that these catalysts facilitate electron transfer from organic molecules, promoting the formation of free radicals and the cleavage of C–C bonds. This enhancement leads to an increased production of short-chain hydrocarbons and a reduction in oxygenates and esters, thereby improving the quality of the shale oil. Further insights from Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations suggest that the catalysts enhance pyrolysis by promoting the formation of carbocations and synergizing with radicals released from the shale, intensifying the pyrolysis process. This study not only elucidates the catalytic mechanisms that enhance oil shale pyrolysis but also offers a promising approach to overcome technological challenges in shale resource recovery, such as high heating costs and low efficiency, paving the way for more efficient and cleaner shale oil production.

ce:section.ce:para: Oil shale, a type of sedimentary rock with high ash content, is rich in combustible organic materials that can be converted into shale oil through pyrolysis [1–4]. As an unconventional hydrocarbon resource, oil shale is considered a vital alternative energy source for the 21st century due to its abundant reserves and substantial development potential [5–7]. According to estimates from the China Petroleum and Chemical Industry Federation, the recoverable shale oil resources in China's major onshore basins are approximately 18.6 billion tonnes, with mid- to low-maturity oil shale being particularly significant [8–11]. However, these shales often present challenges such as viscous liquid hydrocarbons, low proportions of mobile oil, and high contents of solid organic matter that hinder the effective application of conventional fracturing techniques, necessitating the use of pyrolysis technologies for extraction. Traditional pyrolysis methods for oil shale face limitations in applicability, environmental impact from open-pit operations, as well as high energy consumption, cost, and low efficiency [12–14]. Catalysts play a crucial role in regulating chemical reactions and reducing energy demands, significantly influencing the yield and distribution of decomposition and cracking products [15,16]. Therefore, the application of catalysts holds significant potential for enhancing the efficiency of extraction technologies for mid- to low-maturity oil shale and advancing their development.

As shale extraction technologies continue to advance, the study of catalytic pyrolysis for mid- to low-maturity shale has gained increasing attention [17]. Currently, pyrolysis catalysts for these shales are primarily applied in surface retorting techniques and include clay minerals, metals, zeolites, and other types of catalysts [18–21]. Transition metal catalysts, in particular, have drawn significant interest due to their low cost and ability to form stable coordination bonds [22,23]. Research has shown that metal oxides such as Fe, Ca, Zn, and Ni, as well as their chlorides, are effective in catalyzing the aromatization of aliphatic hydrocarbons to aromatic hydrocarbons, facilitating secondary cracking of shale oil, and reducing the activation energy for pyrolysis [24,25]. However, the catalytic efficiency of single-transition metal salts remains limited. To enhance their performance, researchers have explored composite and doping techniques [26]. For example, Jin and colleagues synthesized a novel Cu@4A zeolite nanocatalyst through metal doping, reducing the pyrolysis activation energy from 190.73 kJ/mol to 147.95 kJ/mol [27]. Despite advancements in catalyst research, current catalysts still face challenges related to insufficient activity and poor stability. In their latest study, the research group led by Changyan Cao and Weiguo Song at the Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, systematically reviewed the progress in understanding factors affecting catalytic performance in specific thermocatalytic processes[28]. They highlighted that adjusting the metal active centers can significantly enhance thermocatalytic efficiency. Carbon quantum dots, as unique zero-dimensional carbon-based materials, have garnered attention due to their ease of surface functionalization, cost-effectiveness, and small particle size[29]. By doping active metals into carbon quantum dots, it is possible to effectively modulate the surface activity of metal active centers and significantly improve the stability of the catalysts [30,31].

To address these challenges, this study synthesized a class of highly efficient and stable transition metal-doped carbon quantum dot (M/CQDs) catalysts using a solvothermal method for the catalytic pyrolysis of shale. Through retorting experiments, we quantitatively assessed the impact of these catalysts on shale pyrolysis yield and product distribution. Additionally, kinetic analyses were conducted to investigate the influence of M/CQDs on the pyrolytic behavior of oil shale, and various analytical techniques were employed to characterize the pyrolysis products. Based on these findings, molecular dynamics simulations were used to explore the catalytic pyrolysis behavior and mechanisms of organic compounds in the shale in greater depth. This comprehensive approach provides new insights for enhancing the efficiency and stability of oil shale pyrolysis technologies.

All the chemicals, including cobalt chloride (CoCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), citric acid (C6H8O7), choline chloride (C5H14ClNO), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

This research emphasizes the examination of oil shale samples procured from the Changqing oilfield. The fundamental attributes of these samples were evaluated in accordance with the national standards GB/T212-2008 and GB/T476-200. A detailed summary of the empirical analysis findings is delineated in Table 1 , while Table 2 showcases the analysis of the ash component.

In this study, all density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted using the first-principles simulation software package, Materials Studio. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the revised Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (rPBE) functional (GGA-rPBE) was employed to describe the electronic interactions. The projected augmented wave (PAW) method was used to describe the valence electrons, with a plane wave basis set and a kinetic energy cutoff of 500 eV. Structural optimizations required residual force and electronic energy differences to be less than −0.05 eV Å−1 and 10–5 eV, respectively. The interaction between cheese root and the catalyst surface was calculated using the Forcite module in combination with the Compass II force field. For model construction, M−O4 was embedded in a graphene structure of 17.16 × 14.87 Å2 as the active center of the catalyst, with a 20 Å vacuum layer introduced to avoid interactions between the catalyst surface and cheese root and to eliminate surface effects.
The preparation process of Co/CQDs catalysts by Solvothermal method [32] is outlined below:

Specifically, 190.12 mg (1 mmol) of citric acid, 139.63 mg (1 mmol) of choline chloride and 32.45 mg (0.25 mmol) of cobalt chloride were dissolved in 50 mL of ethanol, and the pH was adjusted to weakly basic using a 5 M solution of sodium hydroxide. The resulting mixture was sonicated for approximately 30 min with ultrasound assistance and then transferred to a 50 mL PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated to 200 °C, and allowed to react for 6 h. The reaction solution was centrifuged and filtered, and the supernatant was dialyzed for 48 h using a 3500 kDa MWCO dialysis bag to remove soluble small molecular impurities. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the target product Co/CQDs. Similarly, Mn/CQDs and Ni/CQDs can be obtained using the same method.

In this experiment, a custom-built dry distillation furnace was used to investigate the influence of M/CQDs catalysts on the pyrolysis behavior of oil shale. The schematic diagram of the apparatus is depicted in Fig. 1 . During the experiment, a mixture of catalyst and oil shale (1 wt% M/CQDs) was loaded into the reactor. Prior to the reaction, nitrogen was purged for 10 min to ensure an oxygen-free environment throughout the process. Subsequently, the temperature was elevated to the desired reaction temperature (ranging from 350 to 550°C, in 50°C increments) at a rate of 10°C/min and maintained for 60 min. Throughout the reaction process, the concentration of the target products in the gaseous phase was determined using gas chromatography. The solid phase products in the reactor were directly weighed to compute the yield of shale semi-coke. An ice-water bath was utilized to condense the generated oil–water mixture, which was then dehydrated using a toluene azeotropic method to obtain the oil yield in the oil–water mixture. The formula for calculating the oil shale yield is as follows (Eq. (1)):
(1)
φ =
m 0
m s
× 100 %
where
φ
was the yield of shale oil.
m o
(g) was the mass of the cracked shale oil, and
m s
(g) was the mass of oil shale. Similarly, the yields of other pyrolysis products (product gas, water and shale semi-coke) can be obtained.

In this study, the Flynn-Wall-Ozawa (F-W-O) method was used to calculate the kinetic parameters associated with the initial pyrolysis stage of oil shale under the influence of a catalyst. The reaction rate due to solid reactants can be described by the following equation:
(2)

By applying the Flynn-Wall-Ozawa (F-W-O) method to Eq. (2), we assume that A, E, and f(α) are independent of the temperature T. Consequently, A and E are also independent of α. Integrating both sides of Eq. (2) subsequently yields:
(3)

represents the rate of temperature rise.

Taking logarithms of both ends of Eq. (3) gives:
(4)

It has been shown that the value of lnp(E/RT) is approximately equal to the following equation when E/RT ≥ 20:
(5)

Then, Eq. (4) can be written as:

RT
It can be seen that for the F-W-O method, the apparent activation energy of the reaction can be found by plotting a linear regression line of x = 1/T versus y= ln(β) based on its slope.

Fig. 2 illustrates the synthetic pathway for the M/CQDs catalyst, where metal doping into carbon quantum dots is achieved through the interaction of citric acid with transition metal ions. Detailed observations of the morphology and lattice structure of the synthesized M/CQDs catalysts were conducted using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The corresponding TEM images displayed in Fig. 2 provide visual evidence of the catalyst's characteristics. These TEM images clearly show that the synthesized M/CQDs catalysts are uniform nanospheres in size and distribution. The particle sizes of various catalyst types, namely Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs, are approximately 6.5 nm, 2.7 nm, and 8.7 nm, respectively. Additionally, the observed lattice fringes of the M/CQDs catalysts, approximately 0.3 nm in width, are consistent with those reported in the literature [33–35], confirming the successful synthesis of the M/CQDs catalysts.

The crystalline structures of M/CQDs (Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs) were thoroughly analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), as presented in Fig. 3 (a). The results demonstrate that the synthesized carbon quantum dot catalysts possess high crystallinity, with a diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.5° matching the (002) plane of graphite. This finding aligns with the standard PDF 75-1621 data [36], confirming the catalysts' graphite-like structural characteristics. Additionally, each catalyst's XRD pattern exhibits characteristic peaks of their respective metal oxides, indicating that transition metals are likely incorporated into the carbon quantum dots in the form of metal oxides [37,38]. Moreover, diffraction peaks and XRD features observed at lower angles (around 10°) suggest the presence of amorphous structures within the CQDs. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy further reveals that the spectra of M/CQDs and CQDs are comparable, indicating that the introduction of metal dopants does not significantly disrupt the carbon quantum framework, as depicted in Fig. 3(b). Moreover, a shift of the C-O infrared absorption peaks towards lower frequencies was observed for Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs, indicating coordination between Mn, Ni, Co, and oxygen atoms. Peaks at 3399, 1609, and 1400 cm−1 reveal the presence of O–H, C = O, and C-N bonds on the structure's surface, enhancing the dispersibility of the carbon dots in water, as illustrated in Fig. 3(c). The zeta potentials of CQDs, Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs were measured as −9.98, −6.31, −7.12, and −5.82 mV, respectively, showing a shift towards more positive values upon metal doping, due to the inherently negative charge of CQDs. Fig. 3(d) presents the Raman spectra of M/CQDs and CQDs, displaying two distinct peaks at (1390 and 1622) cm−1 for the D and G bands respectively. The G band (sp2 hybridization) indicates graphitization related to N-CQDs, while the D band (sp3 hybridization) indicates the degree of defects and functionalization, with a noticeable increase in defects upon metal doping. Additionally, the UV spectra of CQDs and M/CQDs (Fig. 3(e)) show strong absorption peaks between 200–250 nm, attributed to the π-π* transitions of the graphitic sp2 domains on the CQDs surface. M/CQDs exhibit UV absorption in the 300–400 nm range, with the strongest peak at 350 nm, typical of n-π* transitions possibly due to conjugation induced by metal doping. Furthermore, these catalysts display significant fluorescence under UV light, as shown in Fig. 3(f). This study successfully synthesizes Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs catalysts, demonstrating their structural and functional properties.

The chemical composition and valence states of M/CQDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as depicted in Fig. 4 . The comprehensive spectra of the M/CQDs catalysts confirmed the presence of C, N, O, and M (Co, Ni, and Mn) elements on their surfaces, thereby verifying the successful integration of metals with carbon quantum dots. Peak fitting of the C1s peaks indicated the presence of C–C, C-N, and C = O bonds on the surface of M/CQDs, consistent with infrared spectroscopy results. Peak fitting of the O 1 s peaks in the M/CQDs catalysts revealed that oxygen primarily exists in the forms of O–H, M−O, and C = O bonds. Notably, the C = O bond in Co/CQDs may break during high-temperature reactions or its characteristic peak intensity could be too low. Further analysis of the metal valence states was conducted through the Co 2p, Ni 2p, and Mn 2p spectra of Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs, respectively. Peaks at 797.42 eV and 781.81 eV correspond to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2; peaks at 856.91 eV and 873.97 eV correspond to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2; peaks at 641.91 eV and 654.22 eV correspond to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2. Based on the literature, the valence states of Co, Ni, and Mn in M/CQDs were identified as + 2, +2, and + 2/+4, respectively. Furthermore, shifts in the binding energies of M 2p and O 1 s suggest changes in the electronic states of metal and oxygen atoms due to coordination during the doping process of metal in carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of pyrolytic behavior and kinetics

ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the study utilized thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) to investigate the catalytic effects of metal-doped carbon quantum dots (M/CQDs) on the pyrolysis of oil shale (1 wt%, 5 °C/min). Fig. 5(a) and 5(b) display the non-isothermal TG and DTG processes of oil shale with various transition metal salts. The pyrolysis of oil shale predominantly occurs in three stages: the initial phase (25–300 °C), where primarily adsorbed and free water is lost from the shale and catalyst surface; the main weight loss phase (300–550 °C), during which kerogen first transforms into pyrolytic bitumen and then further decomposes into oil and gas; and the final stage (above 550 °C), characterized by coke formation and the decomposition of minerals in the oil shale. The TG and DTG curves of oil shale samples catalyzed with transition metal salts showed no significant differences, with only a slight increase in the rate of pyrolysis before 400 °C compared to the uncatalyzed samples. This indicates a limited catalytic effect of transition metals, possibly due to the instability and deactivation of these metal salts under high temperatures. Fig. 5(c) and 5(d) present the TG-DTG curves for oil shale catalyzed with M/CQDs, revealing distinct differences in conversion behavior under the influence of these catalysts compared to non-catalyzed samples. The incorporation of M/CQDs significantly accelerated the decomposition process of oil shale. Notably, the weight loss rate of the shale samples increased from 14.43 % to 19.28 % with the addition of the catalyst, highlighting the catalytic potency of M/CQDs. Furthermore, the onset temperature of oil shale decomposition was significantly lowered, and the entire pyrolysis process was markedly accelerated. The DTG curves confirmed that M/CQDs effectively reduced both the initial temperature of decomposition and the peak temperature of the pyrolysis rate. Specifically, Ni/CQDs, Co/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs reduced the initial pyrolysis temperature of the shale by 64 °C, 62 °C, and 94 °C, respectively, and lowered the peak temperature of the pyrolysis rate by 16 °C, 17 °C, and 18 °C. These results demonstrate that M/CQDs are highly effective catalysts for the pyrolysis of oil shale, enhancing the efficiency of the process and suggesting the potential for these catalysts to maintain stability and protect the catalytic centers during pyrolysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of oil shale pyrolysis products
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 illustrates the yield of oil shale samples with and without the addition of various catalysts at different temperatures. Each sample was tested three times, and the results presented are the average values of these trials. The data indicate that within the temperature range of 350–550 °C, the yield of shale oil steadily increases as the pyrolysis temperature rises. Notably, the inclusion of M/CQDs catalysts during the pyrolysis process significantly enhances the yield of shale oil, suggesting that M/CQDs catalysts facilitate the thermal decomposition of organic matter in oil shale. The graph shows that beyond 500 °C, further increases in temperature do not significantly affect the yield of pyrolysis products from oil shale. This phenomenon can be attributed to the incomplete decomposition of large organic molecules in the shale at temperatures below 500°C. As the pyrolysis temperature increases, the decomposition of these large molecules intensifies, releasing more shale oil and gas. At a pyrolysis temperature of 500 °C, the complete thermal decomposition of large organic molecules, such as kerogen, occurs, maximizing the yield of shale oil. However, at temperatures above 500 °C, the shale oil and gas produced may undergo secondary cracking and oligomerization reactions, which can reduce the yield of shale oil [22]. This suggests that the introduction of M/CQDs catalysts reduces the temperature corresponding to the maximum oil yield to approximately 500 °C. In contrast, the maximum oil yield for traditional ore-based catalysts occurs around 550 °C [17], and for transition metal salt catalysts, around 520 °C [22]. This highlights the significant advantage of M/CQDs catalysts in enhancing catalytic activity and lowering the reaction activation energy.

Table 3 provides a comparative analysis of the yields of pyrolysis products from oil shale samples at 500 °C, both with and without the addition of different catalysts. Data indicate that the introduction of M/CQDs catalysts significantly enhances the yield of shale oil while effectively reducing the formation rate of semicoke. At the experimental temperature, the use of Ni/CQDs, Mn/CQDs, and Co/CQDs catalysts increased shale oil production by 13.3 %, 19.8 %, and 23.1 %, respectively. Compared to the novel NiFe-LDH catalyst studied by Liu and colleagues [39], the oil yield improvement is approximately 10 %, demonstrating the superior efficiency of M/CQDs catalysts in organic matter catalytic cracking. Furthermore, the addition of M/CQDs catalysts resulted in a decrease in shale gas production, indicating that M/CQDs can alter the chemical reaction pathways, particularly by promoting the conversion of larger organic molecules into smaller oil-like molecules instead of gas molecules.

The impact of M/CQDs catalysts on the pyrolysis behavior of oil shale was investigated using the FWO kinetic model, focusing on parameters such as E and A, with detailed results presented in Table 4 . Previous studies have indicated that the primary pyrolysis of organic matter in oil shale occurs during the second stage of pyrolysis; hence, our experimental analysis concentrated on this phase. The correlation coefficient (R2) between lnβ and 1/T demonstrated a strong linear relationship within the selected range, with regression coefficients all above 0.90, indicating high precision of the chosen kinetic model. The effectiveness of the catalysts was ranked based on the reduction in activation energy: Mn/CQDs > Co/CQDs > Ni/CQDs, which reduced the activation energy of oil shale pyrolysis by 49, 42, and 29 kJ/mol, respectively. During the second stage of pyrolysis, the primary process involves the cleavage of intermediate pitches into smaller molecular oils. This stage requires the action of carbocations, and the M/CQDs catalysts facilitate the formation of these carbocations, thereby lowering the activation energy required for the reaction.

Fig. 7 illustrates the composition of gases at different temperatures during the oil shale pyrolysis process, conducted both with and without catalysts. The key gases yielded during the catalytic pyrolysis of oil shale include H2, CO, CO2, CH4, C2H4, and C2H6. The concentration of each gas component amplifies with temperature, peaking at approximately 500°C, which corroborates our earlier observations. The gases C2H4 and C2H6 in the oil shale pyrolysis gas are produced from aliphatic hydrocarbons cracking and aromatic rings' side chain cracking. In contrast, H2, CO, CO2, and CH4 primarily derive from the dehydrogenation of oil shale's aromatic rings, aliphatic alkanes cyclization, and carboxyl and ether compounds cracking. The relative content of gases like C2H4 and C2H6 notably increases with the introduction of catalysts, suggesting that the catalysts facilitate the breakage of aliphatic and aromatic side chains during organic matter pyrolysis. The rise in the pyrolysis content of H2, CO, CO2, and CH4 is attributed to the interaction of free radicals generated by kerogen and carbon quantum dots during the co-pyrolysis of shale and carbon quantum dots. Comparison with shale gas produced by traditional catalysts reveals that M/CQDs catalysts effectively enhance the yield of shale gas [23,27]. Notably, the proportion of light hydrocarbons to CO2 increases significantly, indicating that M/CQDs catalysts have a stronger capability to facilitate the cleavage of C–C and C-O bonds. This, in turn, accelerates the breakdown of larger organic molecules and the generation of CO2.

To enhance our understanding of the catalytic reaction pathways, analyses and characterizations were conducted on the semi-coke and shale oil produced after catalytic pyrolysis at 500 °C using Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs catalysts. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the infrared spectrum of the semi-coke produced by pyrolysis reveals primary absorption peaks at 2851 cm−1 and 2926 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of aliphatic C–H bonds, indicating a predominance of aliphatic substances in the shale organic matter. After pyrolysis, the intensity of these peaks significantly decreases, suggesting nearly complete thermal decomposition of the organics at 500 °C. The introduction of M/CQDs catalysts further reduces the intensity of the C–H peaks, indicating an acceleration in the thermal decomposition of organics. Fig. 8(b) presents the Fourier-transform infrared spectra of shale oil produced under different catalytic conditions. The spectra show minimal variation, indicating that the catalysts have a negligible impact on the types of functional groups in the shale oil. All spectra display a peak at 1704 cm−1, indicative of ketones, aldehydes, or esters, while peaks at 738 and 907 cm−1 suggest the presence of mono-, poly-, and substituted aromatic hydrocarbons. The addition of catalysts amplifies the absorption peaks at 738 and 907 cm−1 and reduces the peak at 1704 cm−1, suggesting an increase in aromatic hydrocarbons and a reduction in oxygen-containing compounds. As illustrated in Fig. 8(c), the introduction of catalysts evidently increases the carbon and hydrogen content in shale oil while decreasing the nitrogen and oxygen content, consistent with the infrared results. Compared to other molecular sieve and transition metal catalysts [23,25], M/CQDs catalysts exhibit significantly enhanced deoxygenation capabilities, achieving levels 2 to 5 times greater. This also indicates that M/CQDs catalysts can effectively improve the quality and performance of the oil. Fig. 8(d) presents the carbon number distribution of shale oil under various pyrolysis conditions. It is evident that the introduction of M/CQDs results in a reduction in the carbon number of aliphatic chains in the produced shale oil. This indicates that the transition metal ions in the M/CQDs catalysts provide Lewis acid sites, acting as activation centers that effectively accelerate the decomposition of long-chain hydrocarbons and further promote the formation of smaller hydrocarbon fragments.

Fig. 9 presents the GC/MS total ion chromatograms of shale oil produced from the rapid pyrolysis of oil shale using different catalysts. Each compound has been qualitatively identified, and its relative abundance was calculated by normalizing the chromatographic peak areas. The major components have been identified and annotated in the figure. The chromatograms reveal significant differences in the composition and abundance of shale oil generated under M/CQDs catalytic conditions compared to those produced without a catalyst. In the absence of a catalyst, the product distribution across retention times is relatively uniform. However, the introduction of M/CQDs catalysts results in a marked increase in the abundance of products within the intermediate retention time range. Compared to shale pyrolysis catalysts used in recent years [27,39], M/CQDs catalysts more effectively enhance the pyrolysis process of shale, facilitating complex reactions such as bond cleavage, cyclization, decarboxylation, isomerization, aromatization, and oligomerization.

Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the impact of M/CQDs catalysts on the composition of organic components in shale oil during pyrolysis. The study demonstrates that the introduction of various M/CQDs (Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs) results in the reduction of oxygenated compounds and esters in shale oil by 8.21 %, 7.66 %, 8.56 % and 2.91 %, 2.80 %, 4.61 %, respectively. Concurrently, there is a significant increase in the production of olefins, aromatic compounds, and alkanes. This indicates that M/CQDs catalysts facilitate the deoxygenation and cracking of organic matter in shale, converting long-chain alkanes into olefins, enhancing selectivity towards aromatics, and promoting the aromatization of olefins. Fig. 10(b) analyzes the effects of different catalysts on the distribution of heterocyclic compounds, particularly oxygen-containing heterocycles. Results show that with the addition of M/CQDs (Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs), the content of heterocyclic compounds decreases by 8.40 %, 3.61 %, and 4.71 %, respectively. This suggests that using M/CQDs effectively reduces the heteroatom content in shale oil, thus improving fuel quality, consistent with previous findings. Fig. 10(c) explores the influence of various catalysts on the distribution of saturated hydrocarbons. Upon introducing M/CQDs catalysts, the total content of saturated hydrocarbons increases, with a shift towards lower carbon numbers. Specifically, the use of Mn/CQDs reduces the average carbon number of saturated hydrocarbons in shale oil from C20-C24 to C17-C20. This indicates that M/CQDs catalysts promote secondary cracking of organic matter in oil shale. This effect may be attributed to the presence of transition metal ions in M/CQDs catalysts, which lower the activation energy of pyrolysis, facilitating the cleavage of C–C bonds in alkane side chains, thus enhancing further cracking of organic materials. Finally, Fig. 10(d) presents the impact of different catalysts on the distribution of unsaturated hydrocarbons. Compared to conditions without catalysts, the introduction of M/CQDs catalysts results in a 1.11 to 1.38 fold increase in unsaturated hydrocarbon yield. In conclusion, compared to other shale catalysts developed in recent years [21,25,26], the primary advantage of M/CQDs catalysts is their ability to initiate the pyrolysis of organic matter by facilitating heteroatom cleavage, accelerating the breakdown of residual large organic molecules, and promoting aromatization reactions. Furthermore, these findings, aside from the changes in heterocyclic content, further confirm the significant role of M/CQDs catalysts in enhancing deoxygenation reactions and the production of unsaturated hydrocarbons during high-temperature decomposition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculations and molecular dynamics simulations
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a deeper understanding of the influence of metal doping on the catalytic mechanisms of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in oil shale pyrolysis, this study employed theoretical calculations to investigate the effects of M/CQDs catalysts. As shown in Fig. 11 (a), models of different M/CQDs catalysts and adsorbed kerogen were constructed. Graphene was used as a simplified model for carbon quantum dots, and transition metals were incorporated in the form of oxides, as confirmed by the characterization of M/CQDs. Additionally, a simplified kerogen model was built based on the repeating units of the Estonian kerogen model, and its adsorption on M/CQDs was simulated. Fig. 11(b) displays the differences in charge density distribution on the M/CQDs models with adsorbed kerogen. The results indicate significant charge transfer phenomena around the metal centers of the adsorbed kerogen molecules, particularly on oxygen atoms adjacent to the metal atoms, where electron density decreases while the charge on hydrogen and carbon atoms increases. These changes suggest that the transition metal-doped carbon quantum dots significantly activate the kerogen molecules, leading to notable alterations in the internal electronic structure of the kerogen. During the interaction between M/CQDs and kerogen, a Lewis acid site formed by the catalyst might accept an electron from organic groups like carboxylic acids, leading to CO2 formation and the generation of free radicals. These radicals could further induce rearrangements, resulting in C–C bond cleavage and the formation of short-chain hydrocarbons. Moreover, the study employed a layered modeling approach, combining the kerogen model with the molecular plane model of Mn/CQDs. Fig. 11(c) illustrates the interaction energies between M/CQDs (including Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs) and kerogen molecules. The results indicate that all interaction energies are negative, signifying a strong attraction between the kerogen molecules and the M/CQDs. Among these, the Mn/CQDs exhibited the strongest interaction with kerogen molecules, with an interaction energy of −11.43 kcal/mol. To further explore the specific interaction mechanisms between M/CQDs and kerogen, the study conducted simulations of Mn/CQDs interacting with kerogen molecules at the optimal pyrolysis temperature of 500 °C. These simulations were performed under constant volume and temperature (NVT) conditions over 100 picoseconds, with conformations saved every 1000 steps. The findings revealed that upon contact and reaction with kerogen molecules, the surface structure of Mn/CQDs transformed from a regular two-dimensional plane to an irregular configuration, with significant changes observed in the O-Mn-O bond angles. Simultaneously, the kerogen molecules themselves underwent substantial conformational changes, including deformation of the central hexagonal ring, molecular condensation, and bending of C–C bonds. These observations confirm the presence of strong interactions between the kerogen molecules and the Mn/CQDs surface. Overall, these results suggest that the presence of M/CQDs facilitates the decomposition of kerogen during the pyrolysis of oil shale.

This study utilized density functional theory (DFT) to conduct an in-depth analysis of the catalytic behavior of transition metal-doped carbon quantum dots (M/CQDs) during the pyrolysis of oil shale. As depicted in Fig. 12 (a), cluster models of Mn/CQDs and a simplified kerogen model were developed to examine their catalytic roles throughout the pyrolysis process. The simulated reactions considered multiple independent carbon chain reaction pathways to streamline the computational efforts [40–42]. The pyrolytic behavior of kerogen, both in the presence and absence of a catalyst, is shown in Fig. 12(b). Additionally, activation energies for these processes were calculated using the Dmol3 module in Materials Studio. It was discovered that the Gibbs free energy change for the cleavage of carbon–oxygen bonds in kerogen molecules was positive when no catalyst was present, indicating that the reaction is non-spontaneous. However, the introduction of the Mn/CQDs catalyst resulted in a negative Gibbs free energy change for the same bond cleavage, as shown in Fig. 12(c), suggesting that the M/CQDs catalyst significantly reduces the reaction barrier and facilitates bond cleavage. These computational findings further confirm the efficacy of M/CQDs catalysts in enhancing the reactivity of kerogen molecules.

This study provides a comprehensive investigation into the reaction mechanisms of oil shale pyrolysis catalyzed by metal-doped carbon quantum dots (M/CQDs), as depicted in Fig. 13 . Through the analysis of catalytic pyrolysis products, catalyst characterization, and theoretical calculations, this research highlights the enhanced catalytic activity of transition metals when doped into carbon dots. Notably, the M/CQDs prevent the agglomeration and migration of metal ions, maintaining catalyst activity and stability at high temperatures. The study identifies two primary mechanisms through which M/CQDs facilitate the pyrolysis of oil shale. First, the doping of metals alters the electronic structure and electron density of the carbon quantum dots. This alteration aids in the transfer of electrons from the catalyst to reactive sites on organic molecules within the oil shale. For instance, M/CQDs can extract an electron from the carboxyl groups of organic substances, creating a Lewis acid. The loss of this electron from the carboxyl group leads to the formation of carbon dioxide and free radicals. These radicals may further induce rearrangements causing C–C bond breakage, resulting in the formation of short-chain hydrocarbons, or they may promote the formation of carbocations. Subsequently, these ions undergo β-scission reactions with neutral molecules or other carbocations to produce low molecular weight hydrocarbons. Secondly, the carbon quantum dots exhibit numerous active groups that interact with the radicals generated during the pyrolysis of oil shale. This interaction reduces the aggregation of large molecular radicals and adjusts the distribution of oxygen in the volatiles produced during pyrolysis, thereby accelerating the formation of carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide, while reducing the oxygen content in the liquid products, thus enhancing the quality of shale oil. Therefore, the catalytic pyrolysis of oil shale in the presence of M/CQDs can be viewed as a mechanism facilitated by electron transfer, co-pyrolysis, catalytic cracking, and rearrangement reactions. The insights gained from this study provide a substantial experimental and theoretical basis for a full understanding of the role of M/CQDs catalysts in the catalytic cracking of shale.

The study successfully synthesized three catalysts—Co/CQDs, Ni/CQDs, and Mn/CQDs—via the solvothermal method and examined their effects on the pyrolysis of oil shale. Kinetic analysis revealed that the introduction of these catalysts significantly reduced the activation energy required for oil shale pyrolysis by 42 kJ/mol, 29 kJ/mol, and 49 kJ/mol, respectively. Additionally, these catalysts effectively lowered the initiation temperature of oil shale pyrolysis by 64 °C, 62 °C, and 94 °C, confirming their role in enhancing the pyrolysis reaction. When introduced at a temperature of 500 °C, these catalysts also notably increased the yield of shale oil by 23.1 %, 13.3 %, and 19.8 %, respectively. Analysis of the catalytic pyrolysis products revealed that the incorporation of M/CQDs catalysts led to a decrease in the production of oxygenates and esters, while increasing the yield of olefins, aromatics, and alkanes. Theoretical calculations and product analysis indicated that the metal-doped carbon quantum dots altered the material's electronic structure, facilitating electron transfer from the catalyst to reactive sites on organic molecules in the oil shale. This promoted the formation of free radicals and the cleavage of C–C bonds, resulting in the production of short-chain free hydrocarbons or low molecular weight hydrocarbons. Furthermore, the active groups on the carbon quantum dots interacted with the radicals in the oil shale, reducing the aggregation of large molecular radicals and adjusting the distribution of oxygen in the volatiles, thereby accelerating the formation of carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide and enhancing the quality of the shale oil. Overall, this research confirms that the synthesized M/CQDs catalysts exhibit excellent catalytic performance and hold significant potential for application in shale catalytic cracking. These findings contribute to understanding the potential of M/CQDs catalysts in achieving efficient and sustainable pyrolysis of oil shale, thereby aiding in the development of more effective and environmentally friendly catalysts for shale oil production.

Li Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jixiang Guo: Visualization, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yu Zhou: Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Xiaojun Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software. Chenhao Gao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Ruiying Xiong: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Hanxuan Song: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Xiangwei Chen: Visualization, Validation. Junjie Luo: Visualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52174047) and Sinopec Project (No. P23138).
